explanation: Elaeis guineensis (palm) kernel oil is pressed from the inner kernel of the oil palm fruit and is high in lauric and myristic fatty acids — a profile similar to coconut oil — giving it solid-at-room-temperature consistency and occlusive emollient properties. It is well-tolerated by most skin types, though its high lauric acid content gives it moderate comedogenic potential on acne-prone skin.

Elaeis guineensis (palm) oil is extracted from the outer flesh of the oil palm fruit and is rich in palmitic and oleic fatty acids with natural beta-carotene and tocotrienols providing antioxidant activity. It is well-tolerated as an emollient, though its palmitic acid content gives it moderate comedogenic potential and its high saturate level makes it a heavier choice than lighter plant oils for oily skin types.

Elettaria cardamomum (cardamom) seed oil is a spice-derived essential oil used for its warm, aromatic scent but is a contact sensitizer containing terpene compounds including limonene and linalool that can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Epilobium angustifolium (fireweed/willow herb) flower/leaf/stem extract is an antioxidant botanical rich in oenothein B, ellagitannins, and gallic acid with documented anti-inflammatory and sebum-regulating properties. It is well-tolerated and is widely used in calming and blemish-targeting formulas for its ability to soothe reactive skin and inhibit inflammatory pathways.

Epilobium fleischeri (alpine willow herb) flower/leaf/stem extract is a high-altitude botanical relative of fireweed, providing ellagitannins and polyphenol antioxidants with anti-inflammatory and skin-soothing properties similar to Epilobium angustifolium. It is well-tolerated and used in calming formulas for reactive and sensitive skin.

Ergosterol is a fungal and plant sterol (provitamin D2) that helps reinforce the skin's lipid barrier and contributes antioxidant activity by scavenging free radicals. It is well-tolerated and used in barrier-supporting and protective formulas alongside other skin-compatible sterols like cholesterol and beta-sitosterol.

Erythritol is a four-carbon sugar alcohol derived from fermentation of glucose, used in skincare as a humectant that draws and retains moisture in the upper skin layers. It is well-tolerated, non-irritating, and non-comedogenic, and is included in hydrating formulas for its moisture-binding properties and light, non-sticky skin feel.

Ethoxydiglycol is a glycol ether solvent used in skincare and fragrance formulas to dissolve poorly water-soluble ingredients including fragrances, retinol, and other actives. It is generally well-tolerated at typical use concentrations and functions primarily as a solubilizer and skin-feel modifier rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Ethyl ascorbyl ether (3-O-ethyl ascorbic acid) is a stable ether-bonded vitamin C derivative that penetrates skin more effectively than water-soluble vitamin C forms and converts to active ascorbic acid in skin cells to deliver antioxidant protection and inhibit melanin synthesis. It is significantly more stable than pure L-ascorbic acid and causes considerably less irritation, making it a practical brightening active for reactive or sensitive skin.

Ethyl hexanediol (2-ethyl-1,3-hexanediol) is a diol used in skincare as a humectant and mild preservative booster. It is generally well-tolerated and is included in some formulas as a solvent and low-sensitization alternative to harsher glycols.

Ethylene brassylate is a synthetic macrocyclic musk fragrance compound used to impart a clean, musky scent to personal care products. While considered more environmentally biodegradable than nitromusks, it is still a synthetic musk with limited long-term skin safety data compared to non-musk fragrance components — those with fragrance sensitivity should approach products containing musk compounds with caution.

Ethylhexyl hydroxystearate is a lightweight ester emollient derived from 2-ethylhexanol and hydroxystearic acid, providing a smooth, non-greasy skin feel with good emollient properties. It is well-tolerated and non-comedogenic, used in moisturizers and sunscreens as a skin-feel enhancer.

Ethylhexyl isononanoate is a lightweight synthetic ester emollient providing a dry, non-greasy skin feel and excellent spreadability. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in lightweight moisturizers and sun care formulas as a comfortable skin-feel enhancer.

Ethylhexyl methoxycinnamate (octinoxate) is the world's most widely used chemical UVB sunscreen filter, providing effective protection against burning rays but raising concerns about contact sensitization, potential endocrine-disrupting activity at systemic levels, and environmental harm to coral reef ecosystems. It is photounstable without stabilizers and can cause contact and photocontact dermatitis on reactive or sensitive skin.

Ethylhexyl palmitate is a lightweight synthetic ester emollient derived from palmitic acid and 2-ethylhexanol, widely used to provide a smooth, spreadable skin feel in moisturizers, foundations, and sun care formulas. It is generally well-tolerated, though it has moderate comedogenic potential that may not suit acne-prone or congestion-prone skin at high concentrations.

Ethylhexyl salicylate (octyl salicylate) is a chemical UVB sunscreen filter that absorbs UV radiation in the 280–320 nm range. It is milder and less sensitizing than octinoxate but provides narrower UV coverage, and is often combined with other UV filters to broaden protection. It is generally well-tolerated, though it belongs to the salicylate chemical class and may cause reactions in aspirin-sensitive individuals.

Ethylhexyl stearate is a lightweight ester emollient derived from stearic acid and 2-ethylhexanol, providing a smooth, non-greasy skin feel and good spreadability in moisturizers and body products. It is well-tolerated and has low comedogenic potential at typical formula concentrations.

Ethylhexyl triazone (octyl triazone) is a photostable UVB sunscreen filter approved in the EU, Japan, and Canada but not yet in the US. It provides strong UVB protection without the photostability issues of octinoxate, and is well-tolerated with a low sensitization profile.

Ethylhexylglycerin is a glycerin-derived multifunctional ingredient used as a mild preservative booster and humectant, inhibiting bacterial growth on skin while drawing moisture to the surface. It is well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and is a standard component of paraben-free preservative systems in modern clean formulas.

Ethylparaben is a short-chain paraben preservative that has been scrutinized for potential weak estrogenic activity in laboratory studies and is a known contact sensitizer in individuals with paraben sensitivity. It is among the more commonly avoided parabens by reactive skin users, though regulatory bodies consider it safe at approved concentrations in finished products.

Eucalyptus globulus (blue gum eucalyptus) provides 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol) and flavonoid antioxidants with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and decongestant properties. In extract form it is generally better-tolerated than the essential oil, though eucalyptol can cause contact sensitivity in reactive individuals — patch-testing is advisable for fragrance-sensitive skin.

Eucalyptus globulus leaf extract provides polyphenol antioxidants and 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol) with antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. In extract form it is more dilute than the essential oil and generally better-tolerated, though eucalyptol can cause contact reactions on highly reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin.

Eugenia caryophyllus (clove) leaf oil is a clove essential oil with very high eugenol content — a potent contact sensitizer and one of the 26 EU fragrance allergens required to be disclosed on cosmetic labels. It frequently triggers allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin and is best avoided entirely in leave-on products for sensitive skin.

Eugenol is a phenylpropanoid found at high concentrations in clove, cinnamon, and many other essential oils, classified as one of 26 known fragrance allergens required to be disclosed by name on EU cosmetic labels. It is among the more potent fragrance sensitizers and can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Euphorbia cerifera (candelilla) wax is a plant-derived wax from the Chihuahuan Desert shrub, used as a vegan alternative to beeswax as a structuring agent and thickener in balms, lip products, and anhydrous formulas. It is considered inert and well-tolerated, providing a firm, smooth texture with lower occlusive potential than beeswax.

Evening primrose extract is derived from Oenothera biennis and provides polyphenol antioxidants alongside the gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) precursor present in the seeds, with free-radical-scavenging and mild anti-inflammatory properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and soothing formulas for its antioxidant and skin-conditioning benefits.

Ferric ammonium ferrocyanide (CI 77510) is a synthetic mineral pigment that produces the deep blue color known as Prussian blue or iron blue, used in cosmetics including eyeshadows and eyeliners. It is inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated — approved for cosmetic use with restrictions on use near the eyes in some jurisdictions.

Ferulic acid is a hydroxycinnamic acid antioxidant found in plant cell walls that powerfully synergizes with vitamins C and E — the combination is documented to double the photoprotection provided by vitamins C and E alone. It is non-irritating, well-tolerated by all skin types, and is a standard component of evidence-based antioxidant serum formulations.

Ficus carica (fig) fruit extract is a botanical antioxidant containing flavonoids, polyphenols, and enzymes with brightening and free-radical-scavenging properties. It is generally well-tolerated, though fig latex (from the unripe fruit) contains ficin enzymes that can cause contact irritation — properly prepared fig fruit extract at typical formula concentrations is considered safe for most skin types.

Fragaria chiloensis (Chilean strawberry) fruit extract is a botanical antioxidant providing anthocyanins, ellagic acid, and vitamin C with free-radical-scavenging and mild brightening properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and tone-evening formulas for its polyphenol antioxidant content.
